title: Overprotection
subtitle: Make the strong point stronger
summary: Defend an important square more than necessary so your pieces coordinate around it.
attribution: Original prose; ideas after Nimzowitsch, My System. No book text reproduced.

steps.title: The strong point anchors the position
steps.markdown: [[overprotection|Overprotection]] means defending an important point with extra force. The pawn on e5 is White's advanced anchor: it controls space, but Black's d6-pawn attacks it.

steps.title: Add a useful defender
steps.markdown: Improve the knight while adding another defender to the e5 anchor.

hints.text: The b2 knight can move toward the center.
From c4, the knight helps guard e5.

feedback.correct: Yes. Nc4 improves the knight and adds another defender to the e5 anchor.
feedback.wrong: Look for the knight move that improves activity and supports e5.

steps.title: Why defend more than once?
steps.markdown: Why can extra defense of a key point be useful rather than passive?

options.label: The defenders become coordinated active pieces
options.explain: Right. Overprotecting a strong point gives your pieces shared purpose and useful squares.

options.label: It means you should never attack
options.explain: Overprotection is not passivity. It prepares activity from a stable center.

options.label: It makes the pawn a queen immediately
options.explain: The benefit is coordination and control, not instant promotion.

steps.title: Build around what matters
steps.markdown: Do not overprotect everything. Pick the square or pawn that gives your position meaning, then let your pieces gather around it.
